title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning vs Long Context
concept: When to ground with retrieval versus bake knowledge into weights versus just stuffing everything into a huge context window.

exercises.prompt: In the step-by-step RAG pipeline from the last lesson, what is the stage where retrieved text chunks are combined with the user's query before being sent to the LLM?
exercises.explain: During context assembly, the system combines the user's original query with the retrieved and reranked document chunks to create the final prompt for the generator.

cards.title: The Great Battle of Architectures
cards.body: Now that we know how a RAG pipeline works step by step, how do we know we actually need it? Why not just retrain the AI, or paste our entire database directly into the prompt? Let's compare these three powerful superpowers.

cards.title: The Three Knowledge Paths
cards.body: Choosing between RAG, fine-tuning, and long context depends on your data. Fine-tuning updates the model's inner brain weights, RAG fetches facts on demand from an external database, and long context fits massive documents directly into the prompt's temporary memory.

cards.title: The Student's Desk
cards.body: Think of fine-tuning as studying for months to pass a medical exam, RAG as bringing an open-book library to the test, and long-context as having a giant desk where you can spread out 50 open textbooks at the same time.

cards.title: Choosing the Right Tool
cards.body: If you want a chatbot to speak in a specific pirate persona, you fine-tune it. If you want it to answer questions about your company's latest HR policy, you use RAG. If you want it to summarize a single 500-page novel you just uploaded, you use a long context window.

cards.title: The 'Needle in a Haystack' Problem
cards.body: Many think a huge context window makes RAG obsolete. However, stuffing millions of words into a prompt is extremely expensive, gets slower with more text, and the AI can still miss details hidden in the middle of all that data.

cards.title: Dynamic Facts vs Permanent Training
cards.body: Compare the cost and frequency of updates between feeding facts into the context window versus baking them into the model's weights.

left.text: Low startup cost, instant updates, but high cost per question because you send data every time.

right.text: High upfront training cost, slow to update, but lower cost per question since the knowledge is built-in.

exercises.prompt: Which approach is best when your source data updates every five minutes and must be reflected in the AI's answers immediately?

options.text: Hardcoding the answer into the system prompt

exercises.explain: RAG is ideal for dynamic data because you can update the vector database instantly without spending hours and money retraining the model's weights.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Unlike fine-tuning, RAG does not change the underlying weights of the LLM.
exercises.explain: Yes! Retrieval-augmented generation keeps the model's core weights frozen, while fine-tuning actively alters those weights to adapt the model to new patterns.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe how to change a model's behavior:
exercises.sentence: If you need to change the style or tone of an AI, use fine-tuning instead of RAG.
exercises.explain: Fine-tuning changes the internal weights of the model, which is ideal for teaching it a specific style, tone, or format, rather than dynamic, changing facts.

exercises.prompt: Identify the claim that sounds like unrealistic AI hype rather than engineering reality:

options.text: Long-context models let you upload entire textbooks, but they get slower and more expensive with every extra page you add.
Fine-tuning is great for teaching a model a specific programming syntax, but bad for frequent factual updates.
Thanks to infinite context windows, database retrieval is completely dead since LLMs now process gigabytes of data instantly for free.

exercises.explain: Option C is typical AI-generated hallucination/marketing fluff that claims a huge context window has zero downsides and automatically solves all real-time retrieval problems with no cost.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer support bot that needs to troubleshoot real-time server logs that change every hour. The dataset is huge, but the user only asks about specific error codes. Which strategy is most cost-effective and accurate?

options.text: Fine-tune the model every night on the new log files
Use RAG to search and retrieve only the relevant log snippets for each query
Stuff the entire year's log history into a 2-million-token context window every time
Use a basic prompt without any external data or tuning

exercises.explain: RAG is the best fit here because it allows the model to access constantly updating, highly specific external files (open-book) without the massive daily cost of retraining weights (fine-tuning) or the slow, expensive processing of millions of tokens on every single query (long-context).
